title: Water surface elevation at Venoco Bridge
text:
How does mean daily water surface elevation change over the course of a single water year at Venoco Bridge?



To answer this question, we will

use functions to manipulate objects or variables
use packages to take advantage of collections of specialized functions
extract variables from data using the $ operator
assign values to objects using the <- operator
connect functions together using the |> operator
write pseudocode to plan a workflow before coding it up
explore data using:

min()
max()
nrow()

wrangle data using:

select()
mutate()
filter()

summarize data using:

group_by(), summarize(), ungroup()

visualize data using:

ggplot() and its associated functions

title: Water surface elevation at Venoco Bridge
text: How does mean daily water surface elevation change over the course of a single water year at Venoco Bridge?

title: Water surface elevation at Venoco Bridge
text:
To answer this question, we will

use functions to manipulate objects or variables
use packages to take advantage of collections of specialized functions
extract variables from data using the $ operator
assign values to objects using the <- operator
connect functions together using the |> operator
write pseudocode to plan a workflow before coding it up
explore data using:

min()
max()

mutate()

title: Water surface elevation at Venoco Bridge
text:
1. Set up
A set up section is a nice location to read/load in packages and/or data that you’ll use later in your script.
It is best practice to start any script with all the materials you will need later on. This includes both your packages and your data.
Start this set up section by reading in the tidyverse package.

library(tidyverse)

Then, read in the data.
Name the object venoco_water. Use the assignment operator (i.e. the left arrow operator) and the function read_csv() to store the values.

venoco_water <- read_csv("venoco-water.csv")







Functions to read in data



There are many functions to read in data. We will use the read_csv() function from the readr package in the tidyverse because it is good at understanding the values that are in each column of a file.

title: Water surface elevation at Venoco Bridge
text:
2. Data exploration
Then, examine at the data by:

clicking on the object in the Environment or running View(venoco_water) in the Console
exploring the variables in the dataset using str(venoco_water) in the Console
determining the object type by running class(venoco_water) in the Console

There are four columns in this dataset:

date, which is a Date in ISO 8601 format
time, which is an hms object (hms stands for hours minutes seconds)
level, which is a numeric
temperature, which is a numeric

How many observations are in this dataset?

nrow(venoco_water)

[1] 31974


There are 31974 observations.
How could you look at all the measurements of water elevation in this dataset?

venoco_water$comp_level_ft



 [1] 0.00656168 1.19750656 1.19422572 1.18110236 1.18438320 1.18438320
 [7] 1.17782152 1.17454068 1.17454068 1.17454068 1.17782152 1.17125984
[13] 1.17125984 1.17125984 1.17782152 1.17782152 1.18766404 1.19094488
[19] 1.18766404 1.18766404 1.18110236 1.17782152 1.17454068 1.18110236
[25] 1.17454068 1.18110236 1.17782152 1.17454068 1.18438320 1.18766404
[31] 1.18438320 1.18766404 1.19750656 1.19094488 1.19750656 1.18438320
[37] 1.19422572 1.19094488 1.18766404 1.18766404 1.18766404 1.17454068
[43] 1.17782152 1.18110236 1.18766404 1.19422572 1.19422572 1.19750656
[49] 1.19422572 1.19750656
 [ reached 'max' / getOption("max.print") -- omitted 31924 entries ]


This is useful, but not that informative. We can figure out some information about water elevation by applying functions.
In this case, we want to know what the lowest measurement of water elevation is.

min(venoco_water$comp_level_ft, na.rm = TRUE)

[1] 0.00656168


But we also remember that the logger is slightly higher than sea level too.
Accounting for that:

min(venoco_water$comp_level_ft, na.rm = TRUE) + 2.96

[1] 2.966562


The lowest water elevation measured at Venoco bridge was 2.97 ft above sea level.
What was the highest measurement of water elevation in the dataset, accounting for the elevation of the logger?

max(venoco_water$comp_level_ft, na.rm = TRUE) + 2.96

[1] 9.859606


The highest water elevation measured at Venoco bridge was 9.86 ft above sea level.

title: Water surface elevation at Venoco Bridge
text:
3. Cleaning the data
This dataset includes a lot of columns and rows that we don’t need.
We can use functions from packages in the tidyverse to help us clean the data.
First, we could use filter() from the dplyr package in the tidyverse to filter the dataset to only include dates from the 2025 water year.

water1 <- filter(
  venoco_water, # starting with venoco_water
  wy == "2025" # only include observations from 2025 water year
)

Then, we could use mutate() (also from dplyr) to create a new column in which the values are water elevations converted to meters.

water2 <- mutate(
  water1, # starting with water1 data frame
  comp_level_m = (comp_level_ft + 2.96) * 0.3048 # adding logger height and converting to meters from ft
)

Then, we could use select() (also from dplyr) to choose the columns we need to answer our guiding question.

water3 <- select(
  water2, # starting with water2 data frame
  date, comp_level_m, temperature # select columns of interest
)

title: Water surface elevation at Venoco Bridge
text:
4. The pipe operator
Creating multiple data frames (water1, water2, water3) as we did above can be tedious. Instead, you can use the pipe operator |> to chain functions together.
You can think of the pipe operator as being a way of saying “and then”.
In the code we will write, we could say that we will:

create a new object called venoco_clean from venoco_water and then…
filter to only include wy that matches 2025 and then…
mutate to create a new column called comp_level_m to add logger elevation to comp_level_ft and convert to meters and then…
select the columns of interest (only date, comp_level_m, and temperature)

This is “pseudocode”. Pseudocode looks different depending on the coding language, but in this class, “pseudocode” will refer to a bulleted list of steps you will later write in code.
Writing pseudocode makes working with data much more straightforward. If you can plan out your code in plain language, it is easier to write the code (and troubleshoot when things go wrong!).

# create a new object from venoco_water
venoco_clean <- venoco_water |> 
  # filter to only include 2025 water year
  filter(wy == "2025") |> 
  # create a new column to convert water elevation in ft to meters
  mutate(comp_level_m = (comp_level_ft + 2.96) * 0.3048) |> 
  # select columns of interest
  select(date, comp_level_m, temperature)

title: Water surface elevation at Venoco Bridge
text:
5. Grouping data
In this dataset, each row is an observation of water level elevation at a particular time on a given day.
What if you want to visualize the mean water level for a given day?
You can add a function called group_by() from the dplyr package which allows you to “group” a data frame.
When you use group_by(), you are essentially telling R: there are “groups” in this data frame that I need to pay attention to.
When you pair group_by() with summarize() (also from dplyr), you can calculate summary statistics (e.g. calculate the mean daily water elevation) by each group (the date).
In this next section, we will:

create a new object called venoco_daily from venoco_clean
group by date to
summarize (calculate) mean daily water level in m
ungroup the dataframe


# create new object from venoco_clean
venoco_daily <- venoco_clean |> 
  # group data frame by date
  group_by(date) |> 
  # calculate mean water level in meters for a given day
  summarize(mean_level_m = mean(comp_level_m, na.rm = TRUE)) |> 
  # ungroup the data frame
  ungroup()

title: Water surface elevation at Venoco Bridge
text:
6. Visualizing data
To answer our question (How does mean daily water surface elevation change over the course of a single water year at Venoco Bridge?), we need to visualize the data in a timeseries plot.
Timeseries plots have time on the x-axis and the variable of interest on the y-axis.
To visualize the data, we’ll use ggplot() and its associated functions, which are all in the ggplot2 package in the tidyverse.
The main parts of creating a plot in ggplot() are:

the global call: telling R that you want to use ggplot() by naming the function
the data and mapping: telling R the data frame you want to use (with the argument data =) and the columns in that data frame to plot (with the argument mapping = and the aes() function for the “aesthetics”)
the geometries: these are the shapes, lines, etc. in the plot. In this case, we’ll use two geometries: geom_point() to represent mean daily water elevation and geom_line() to represent connections between each day.


# base layer: ggplot
ggplot(data = venoco_daily,
       # x-axis should be date
       # y-axis should be mean daily water elevation
       mapping = aes(x = date,
                     y = mean_level_m)) +
  # first layer: points
  geom_point(color = "#287948") +
  # second layer: line
  geom_line(color = "#287948") +
  # relabeling x- and y-axes, adding title
  labs(x = "Date",
       y = "Mean daily water elevation (m)",
       title = "Water year 2025 (01 October 2024 - 30 September 2025)") +
  # using theme that is different from default
  theme_bw()

title: Water surface elevation at Venoco Bridge
text:
7. Understanding trends
What seasonal trends can you see?
Highs: February, March, April
Declines: April to August
More increases: August, September

title: Water surface elevation at Venoco Bridge
text:
8. Understanding warnings
When you run the code to make the plot, you get a warning.
That warning means there is a missing value in your dataset that can’t be plotted (because it’s missing).
You can use the is.na() function to determine which values are missing.

is.na(venoco_daily$mean_level_m)

 [1] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
[13] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
[25] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
[37] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
[49] FALSE FALSE
 [ reached 'max' / getOption("max.print") -- omitted 263 entries ]


That’s not super helpful.

venoco_daily |> 
  filter(is.na(mean_level_m))

# A tibble: 1 × 2
  date       mean_level_m
  <date>            <dbl>
1 2025-09-03          NaN


The missing value is from 2025-09-03.
END OF CLASS MATERIALS

title: Using the virtual machine
text:
For this class, we have a virtual machine: it allows you to run R and RStudio in a browser (e.g. Google Chrome, Safari, Firefox). Find the virtual machine here!
The benefits of using the virtual machine are plenty:

you don’t have to download R, RStudio, and Quarto

the versions of all the software you need are updated

the packages you need for the class are already installed

you can download everything you’ve worked on

and more!
The one con is that you do need to be connected to the internet. But compared to the benefits, this is hopefully not a major hurdle.
Basically, if you’re having any issues with your versions of R, RStudio, or Quarto, try running your code on the virtual machine.

title: Using the virtual machine
text:
For this class, we have a virtual machine: it allows you to run R and RStudio in a browser (e.g. Google Chrome, Safari, Firefox). Find the virtual machine here!
The benefits of using the virtual machine are plenty:

you don’t have to download R, RStudio, and Quarto

the versions of all the software you need are updated

the packages you need for the class are already installed

you can download everything you’ve worked on

and more!
The one con is that you do need to be connected to the internet. But compared to the benefits, this is hopefully not a major hurdle.
Basically, if you’re having any issues with your versions of R, RStudio, or Quarto, try running your code on the virtual machine.

title: Using the virtual machine
section: 2. Logging in and opening things up
text:
2. Logging in and opening things up
Once you open up the virtual machine, you’ll be asked to log in. Use your UCSB email to do that. You should then get a screen that looks like this:
 Click RStudio.
You should then see a screen that looks exactly like an RStudio screen!

title: Using the virtual machine
text:
3. Setting up
If you’re opening this up for the first time, do task 5 in the Getting set up guide called “Change your settings”: Change your settings.
Additionally, you’ll want to set up a new folder for your ENVS 193DD materials. Make a new folder called ENVS-193DD by clicking on the “Folder” button in the Files tab in the bottom right.

title: Using the virtual machine
text:
4. Getting files into the machine
Download the zipped file of workshop materials from Canvas. Hit the Upload button (yellow arrow pointing up against a white paper). You should see a window that looks like this:

Hit Choose file and select the .zip file.
The machine will automatically unzip the file and create a new folder with all the file contents.

title: Using the virtual machine
text:
5. Creating and using Rprojects
We’re going to create a lot of Rprojects in this class to get used to it. You can create a project in an existing directory (aka folder) in the same way that you would in the desktop version of RStudio. Go to the button in the top left that says Project: (None) and click. Hit New Project.
 Then, select the “Existing Directory” option.

Choose the directory you want to create a project in. Once you click “Browse”, you’ll see a box that looks like this:

Double click the folders until you get to the one you want (in this example, I went from ENVS-193DS > ENVS-193DS_workshop-01).
You can confirm that you chose the right folder once you hit “Choose”:

Hit “Create Project”.
You should now see the new Rproject in two locations: 1) in the upper right corner and 2) in the list of files in your directory.

Once you’re done working in your project, you can close the project by going to Project > Close Project.

If you need to open your project again, navigate to the folder using the Files tab in the bottom right corner. Click on the .Rproj file. You will then see a window that looks like this:

Hit “Yes” to open the project.

title: Using the virtual machine
text:
6. Downloading your files
If you want to hold onto your files on your computer (for example, once the class ends), you can download a whole directory. Click on the folder you want to download and go to More > Export in the lower right pane.

The machine will download the whole folder as a .zip file, which you can then unzip on your computer.

title: Guidelines and policies on using AI
text: Generative AI tools like ChatGPT, Claude, Copilot, etc. can help solve a lot of coding problems: if you’re stuck and don’t know where to start, if you have code that keeps giving you an error, etc. - most of these issues can be solved using AI. The key to effective use of generative AI tools in environmental studies is to be honest about its use.

title: Guidelines and policies on using AI
text: Generative AI tools like ChatGPT, Claude, Copilot, etc. can help solve a lot of coding problems: if you’re stuck and don’t know where to start, if you have code that keeps giving you an error, etc. - most of these issues can be solved using AI. The key to effective use of generative AI tools in environmental studies is to be honest about its use.

title: Guidelines and policies on using AI
section: Why wouldn’t you use AI in this class?
text:
Why wouldn’t you use AI in this class?
If you’re just starting out learning how to code, we recommend not using generative AI tools. You will likely retain a lot more information by googling, asking for help from classmates, meeting with An, or literally doing anything other than using a generative AI tool. In our experience, the conversations and discussions around materials and the struggle of sifting through a lot of information to try different things out is really effective for retaining information.
Additionally, while gen AI can quickly come up with code, it’s not always what you would expect, or what we expect to see in your assignments. It’s straightforward to see if it works - if the output is what you want, then the code works - but there are specifics in each assignment that you as a student enrolled in the class will know that an AI tool will not.

title: Guidelines and policies on using AI
section: If I want to use AI, what am I allowed to do?
text:
If I want to use AI, what am I allowed to do?
If you want to use an AI tool, you may use it to:

To brainstorm or clarify your understanding of the assignment (e.g. “I have this idea but I’m not sure if it’s right. Can you help me figure out if it applies to this assignment?”)

To outline your approach or plan your workflow (e.g. “Can you help me figure out how to start this problem?”)

To clarify or deepen understanding of technical concepts (e.g. “What does this function do in my use case?”)

To generate or refine code snippets (e.g. “I wrote this code but I think it’s too long. How can I condense it?”)

To debug or troubleshoot code (e.g. “I wrote this code but it’s not working. What’s wrong with it?”)

To edit or improve the clarity, structure, or tone of my written responses (e.g. “I wrote this text to summarize a test but it’s too long. Can you help me shorten it to 1-2 sentences?”)

To check my reasoning, results, or interpretation of findings (e.g. “I interpreted a cleaning workflow in this way. Is my interpretation correct?”)

title: Guidelines and policies on using AI
section: What am I not allowed to do?
text:
What am I not allowed to do?
You are not allowed to use generative AI to produce new code for you to copy-paste into your assignments. Additionally, you may not use AI to generate new writing for you. For example, these prompts would not be allowed:

“Write me code to visualize a boxplot.”

“Write the description of this code.”

All code and writing in your assignments should be generated by you alone to start out with.

title: Guidelines and policies on using AI
section: I’m ready to use AI on my assignments. What should I do when I submit?
text:
I’m ready to use AI on my assignments. What should I do when I submit?
All assignments need to include a generative AI statement of use (form here).
Keep in mind that unreported or disallowed use of AI tools will result in an incomplete mark on an assignment and the assignment will not be eligible for revision or resubmission.

title: Guidelines and policies on using AI
section: I’m not sure if what I’m using AI for is allowed. What do I do?
text:
I’m not sure if what I’m using AI for is allowed. What do I do?
Talk to An!

title: Guidelines and policies on using AI
section: I’m troubled about AI use, because of the environmental impacts, disadvantages, brain deterioration, etc. What do I do?
text:
I’m troubled about AI use, because of the environmental impacts, disadvantages, brain deterioration, etc. What do I do?
Again, talk to An! I am here to help navigate this new landscape, and figure out a plan that works for you, whether that’s sparingly using AI, not using it at all and relying on other resources, etc.

title: Individual assignment 1
text:
Fill in your name in the individual-assignment-template.R script.
File > Save as > lastname-firstname_individual-assignment-01.R.
Read in your packages.

Read in your data.

Copy paste any code you need from class into your assignment script.







Practice your own editorial judgement!





You will not need all the code from class to do your assignment.
You will need to pick and choose the components that are relevant to the assignment.
If you copy-paste your entire class script into your homework, you will need to revise your assignment!




Start your homework!

title: Individual assignment 1
text:
1. Exploring missing data

a. Write pseudocode
In class, we practiced writing “pseudocode” to group and summarize the data. Write pseudocode to calculate the number of missing water elevation values from 2025-09-03.
Remember that pseudocode should not include any actual code. It should be plain language in the format of code.


b. Code up your pseudocode.
Write the code that corresponds to the pseudocode that you wrote in part a.
Annotate all your code.


edited 2026-04-02: took out requirement to show output (though output does have to be correct!)


c. Number of missing values
In 1 sentence, state how many missing values there are for water elevation on 2025-09-03.

title: Individual assignment 1
text:
2. Exploration of temperature data
For this exercise, be sure you are looking at venoco_water using View(venoco_water).
Use the column manipulations to answer these questions.

a. Lowest temperature
On what date and at what time was the lowest temperature taken? What was the lowest temperature?
Express all numbers with units as needed.


b. Highest temperature
On what date and at what time was the highest temperature taken? What was the highest temperature?
Express all numbers with units as needed.

title: Individual assignment 1
text:
3. Another visualization

a. Write pseudocode to clean and summarize data
Write pseudocode to calculate the mean daily temperature at Venoco Bridge.


b. Code up your pseudocode.
Code the pseudocode you wrote in part a.


c. Write pseudocode to visualize data
Write pseudocode to visualize mean daily temperature through time.
Use point and line geometries.
Make sure your pseudocode includes lines for labelling x- and y-axes.


d. Code up your pseudocode
Make a visualization of temperature through time following the pseudocode you wrote in part a.
Annotate each function in your code to demonstrate your understanding of what you’ve written.


e. Comparing temporal trends
Does temperature follow similar temporal trends as water elevation? Why or why not?
Compare the visual components of each plot to justify your answer.

title: Individual assignment 1
text:
4. Dealing with dates

a. Types of objects
The earliest observation recorded in the dataset was from 2024-11-13. Run str("2024-11-13") in the Console.
What type of object is this?
Reference specific components of the output of str("2024-11-13") to justify your response.


b. Other types of objects
Now, run str(as.Date("2024-11-13")) in the Console.
In one sentence, describe what type of object is this. Use the specific components of the output of str(as.Date("2024-11-13")) to justify your response.


c. Working with a new dataset
Read in the precipitation data using the following code in your set up section:

precip <- read_csv("NOAA_daily_summaries_USW00053152_full_2025-12-09.csv")

List the variables and what values they contain in a bulleted list.


d. Write pseudocode to filter the dataset
Write pseudocode to create a new object called precip_filtered to filter the dataset to only include precipitation readings from after 2024-09-30 and before 2025-10-01 (the 2025 water year).


e. Code up your pseudocode
Code your pseudocode that you wrote in part d. 
Annotate all lines.

Try using as.Date() and filter() together for this problem.



f. Connecting information
Look at the precip_filtered data frame. Find the column that corresponds to precipitation in inches called PRCP.
What three dates in the precip_filtered data frame likely correspond to peaks in water elevation in (m) from the figure we made in class?
Be specific by stating the date and the amount of precipitation in inches, and which peak the precipitation date connects to (e.g. the peak in [insert month here] likely corresponds to precipitation on [this date], which was [insert amount in inches here]).

title: Getting set up
text:
Optional check in due on Monday 30 March (Week 1) at 11:59 PM
In this class, we’ll be using R and RStudio to code up our statistical analyses. Walk through these steps to make sure you have both programs on your computer and that everything is working properly. You need to make sure all tasks are completed before workshop on Friday.

title: Getting set up
section: Task 1. Set up a folder for class materials
text:
Task 1. Set up a folder for class materials
Using R/RStudio requires you to know how your computer is organized and where your files are. For now, we’ll want to set up a folder in your computer called ENVS-193DD (note no spaces in the folder name).
All operating systems are different, but make sure that your folder is not in the “iCloud”, “OneDrive”, or “Google Drive” folders in your computer. Basically, you want to be sure that you can get from your “root” directory (i.e. your actual computer hard drive) to the folder you’re using.
You can check this using the file path, or the folders you would need to open to get to the folder called ENVS-193DD. One example for MacOS is below, where the file path is shown at the bottom of the pane:











Displaying a file path





The easiest way to know where a file or folder is on your computer is to show the file path.
If you are using Mac OS, see these directions under “Show the path to a file or folder” to display the file path in the Finder window.
If you are using Windows 10 or 11, see this video with directions to show the file path in File Explorer.

title: Getting set up
text:
Task 2. Install R.
Go to cran.rstudio.com. Choose the correct download for your operating system!

title: Getting set up
text:
Task 3. Install RStudio (and optionally Quarto)
Go to posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop. Click on the button under “2: Install RStudio”. It should automatically show the correct version for your operating system.











Installing Quarto





You only need to install Quarto if you are running RStudio version 2022.07.1 or earlier. If you are installing a later version of RStudio, you do not need to install Quarto.
Follow the instructions for installing Quarto here.
I (An) recommend that you install an new version of RStudio instead of installing Quarto, if it is possible on your computer.
If you cannot install Quarto on your computer (because it doesn’t work with your operating system, etc.), you may need to use the virtual machine.

title: Getting set up
text:
Task 4. Open RStudio
When we say we’re “using R” in the class, what we’re really using is RStudio, which is a graphical user interface (GUI) for R (the language). Basically, we’re never going to open up “R”, but we’ll always open up “RStudio”.
Open RStudio on your computer.

title: Getting set up
text:
Task 5. Change your settings
You can change some settings to make your life easier when working in RStudio. The following steps are all done in the same menu. Go to Tools > Global Options.

Change your color scheme.
You don’t have to stick with the boring RStudio color scheme! Go to Appearance and select the color scheme you want.





Click apply before moving on!


Change your workspace save settings.
Forming good data science habits starts from the beginning. Changing your workspace save settings will allow you to start with a clean, blank slate every time you open RStudio.
Go to the General tab. Make sure that:

“Restore most recently opened project at startup” is unchecked

“Restore previously open source documents at startup” is unchecked

“Restore .Rdata into workspace at startup” is unchecked

“Save workspace to .RData on exit” is “Never”

“Always save history (even when not saving .RData)” is unchecked







Some of these may already be checked! If so, great! Double check everything though!











Click apply before moving on!


Change your code editing settings.
Then, go to the Code tab. Under editing, make sure:

“Insert spaces for Tab” is checked

“Auto-detect code indentation” is checked

“Insert matching parens/quotes” is checked

“Use native pipe operator, |>” is checked

“Auto-indent code after paste” is checked

“Vertically align arguments in auto-indent” is checked

“Continue comment when inserting new line” is checked

“Enable hyperlink highlighting in editor” is checked

“Enable code snippets” is checked






Click apply before moving on!


Change your display settings.
Then, go to Display (still under the Code tab). Make sure:

“Highlight selected word” is checked

“Show line numbers” is checked

“Show margin” is checked

“Soft-wrap source files” is checked

“Blinking cursor” (if you want it) is checked

“Enable preview of named and hexadecimal colors” is checked

“Use rainbow parentheses” is checked






Click apply before moving on!


Change your Markdown settings.
Then, go to the R Markdown tab. Under basic, make sure the following is checked:

“Soft-wrap R Markdown files” is checked

“Show output inline for all R Markdown documents” is unchecked

“Show output preview in:” selection is “Viewer Pane”

“Show document outline by default” is checked






Click apply before moving on!


Change your visual settings.
Under visual (still under the R Markdown tab), make sure:

“Use visual editor by default for new documents” is unchecked

“Show document outline by default” is checked






Click apply to save all your changes! Do not move on to the next task without hitting apply!

title: Getting set up
section: Task 6. Test out installing a package
text:
Task 6. Test out installing a package
Packages are the best part of using R. We’ll talk more about what packages are in workshop, but for now try installing a package. Go to your Console (the bottom left pane in the RStudio window), and type (or copy paste) install.packages("tidyverse"). Hit Enter.
You should get a message that looks something like this:











Operating system differences





The database that holds all these packages will automatically detect which version you need based on your operating system. Don’t worry if your output message doesn’t look exactly the same as the one here - just as long as you get something like “The downloaded binary packages are in…”, you’ve probably got the package installed.

title: Getting set up
section: Task 7. Test out reading in a package
text:
Task 7. Test out reading in a package
Now you’ve installed a package, but you want to make sure you can actually run it. Again in the Console (the bottom left pane), type library(tidyverse) and hit Enter.
You should get a message that looks something like this:

title: Getting set up
section: Task 8. Install a small package in the Terminal
text:
Task 8. Install a small package in the Terminal
In this class, we will use a file format called .qmd to integrate code and plain text into a document.
To use .qmd files and turn them into .pdf files, you will need an additional piece of software.
In the Terminal tab (to the right of the console), type the following:
quarto install tinytex
Hit enter to run that line.





Depending on whether or not you have installed tinytex before, you may see different messages.
On my (An’s) computer, I see the message tinytex is already installed and up to date. You may see a different message, or be prompted to do different things. That is ok!

title: Getting set up
section: Task 9. Take a screenshot of your set up.
text:
Task 9. Take a screenshot of your set up.

Take a screenshot of your RStudio window with the Console showing
Your screenshot should look something like this:











Double check your screenshot!





Take a screenshot of your whole RStudio window. Make sure that the messages in the orange box (above) are visible in your screenshot! Otherwise we will not be able to troubleshoot whatever issues you are having with installation (if you are actually having any).





Take a screenshot of your RStudio window with the Terminal showing
Your screenshot should look something like this:











Double check your screenshot!





Again, take a screenshot of your whole RStudio window. Make sure that the messages in the orange box (above) are visible in your screenshot! Otherwise we will not be able to troubleshoot whatever issues you are having with installation (if you are actually having any).





Take a screenshot of your ENVS-193DD folder
Lastly, take a screenshot of your ‘ENVS-193DD’ folder set up from task 1. It should be evident what the file path is, either from a path at the bottom of the window or on the side. It doesn’t matter where the file path appears in your screenshot as long as you can tell where the folder is. Your screenshot should look something like this:





Upload all three screenshots to Canvas for your optional check-in.

title: Getting set up
text:
Triple checking your set up
If you would like to verify your set up or get help, come see An on Tuesday the 31st of March between 9:30 AM - 12 PM in Bren 4017.

title: Format for resubmission cover letter
text:
In a document, put the name of the assignment you want to resubmit and “resubmission cover letter” in the title (e.g. “Individual assignment 2 resubmission cover letter”).
For each component that you want to redo for the possibility of getting full marks, you will need to articulate (in 1-2 sentences):

what you got wrong
what you need to do to fix it
why that fix would improve the quality of your work (independently of the grade you received)

For example, if you got “partial marks” for multiple plots where you had overlapping axis labels, a reasonable request would be:

My x-axis labels for Problem 2a, 3b, and 5c were overlapping.
For 2a, I need to either spread out the x-axis or fix the way I read in the data to make sure the columns are correct (so that the date column is read in as a date rather than a character). For 3b and 5c, I need to create a line break in the axis label.
Making sure the x-axis labels don’t overlap improves the figure because the text would be readable.
